nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coral reefs at approximately 2-10 m depth, living in close association with the host anemones Entacmaea quadricolor and Heteractis crispa.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Central Pacific reef; coral reef areas with hard substrate hosting bubble-tip and leathery sea anemones.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs
open mid-water swimming space to establish territory
host anemone optional (Entacmaea quadricolor bubble-tip anemone preferred, Heteractis crispa also accepted)

diet.notes: Generally accepts most prepared marine fare. A varied diet of frozen mysis, brine shrimp, marine pellets, flake foods and herbivore preparations is commonly recommended. In the wild has been documented eating zooplankton, small crustaceans, algae and polychaete worms.

goodWith: peaceful and semi-aggressive reef fish of similar or smaller size
reef-safe invertebrates including shrimps and snails
avoidWith: other clownfish species in the same tank
small timid species that can be bullied at feeding
large predatory fish that may eat smaller clownfish

summary: A hardy reef-safe Central Pacific clownfish in the Tomato Complex, with a deep reddish-orange body and a single bright white head bar. Native to Fiji, Tonga and American Samoa; commonly hosted by bubble-tip anemones.
careNotes: Amphiprion barberi was formally described by Allen, Drew & Kaufman in 2008; before that, Fiji/Tonga/Samoa populations had often been sold as the Australian Clownfish (A. rubrocinctus) or mistaken for a color variant of the Cinnamon Clownfish (A. melanopus). It is a member of the Tomato Complex and shares that group's bold, territorial disposition: tends to be peaceful toward unrelated tankmates but can be aggressively territorial toward other clownfish or similarly shaped damselfish. Best kept singly or as a single bonded pair; do not mix with other clownfish species. Like all clownfish it is a protandrous (male-first) sequential hermaphrodite — in a bonded pair the larger fish is the female and the smaller is the functional male. Host anemones are not required in captivity, but it readily associates with Entacmaea quadricolor (bubble-tip) and Heteractis crispa when offered. Adult-size reports vary across sources (FishBase 8.6 cm SL; LiveAquaria ~8.9 cm / 3.5 in; Sea & Reef 11.5 cm / 4.5 in) — the 8-11 cm range here is the cross-source average. Tank-size guidance also varies (LiveAquaria 30 US gal / ~113 L for a single pair); 150 L was chosen as a cautious round-up that comfortably houses a pair of this stockier, more territorial Tomato Complex clownfish with a host anemone and reef rockwork. Captive-bred specimens (now widely available from ORA and Sea & Reef) are generally easier to acclimate and accept prepared foods readily.
breedingNotes: Has been bred in captivity, with the first commercial captive-bred specimens released by Sea & Reef in 2012; ORA also produces this species at scale. Generally considered moderate difficulty for an experienced aquarist — comparable to other tomato-complex clownfish. Egg-layer that deposits demersal eggs on a cleaned flat surface (typically near the host anemone); the male guards and aerates the clutch. Nest sizes of 500+ eggs have been reported. Eggs hatch in roughly 8-11 days depending on temperature; larvae require rotifers followed by enriched newly-hatched brine shrimp in a dedicated rearing tank.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary species page (Captive-Bred Barberi Clownfish). The listed root URL https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/clownfish did not surface this species on the catalog page, but the canonical product page was located via site search.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Allen, Drew & Kaufman, 2008), family (Pomacentridae), max size (8.6 cm SL), preferred temperature range (25.8-29°C mean 27°C), Central Pacific distribution (Fiji, Tonga, American Samoa), depth range (2-10 m), and host-anemone associations (Entacmaea quadricolor, Heteractis crispa).

additional.name: ORA (Oceans, Reefs & Aquariums)
additional.notes: Third-source confirmation of Tomato Complex placement, white-head-bar coloration that distinguishes A. barberi from A. melanopus (Cinnamon) and A. frenatus (Tomato), and historical taxonomic confusion with A. rubrocinctus. Notes nest sizes of 500+ eggs are common.

additional.name: Sea & Reef Aquaculture
additional.notes: Fourth-source confirmation of bold/territorial temperament toward conspecifics and clownfish in general, peaceful behaviour toward other species, host anemones (bubble-tip and magnificent), and omnivorous diet. Reports a larger maximum size (11.5 cm / 4.5 in) than FishBase or LiveAquaria; used in the adult-size cross-source average.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary in source-urls.json for fish-saltwater slice. Per-species wiki page not separately verified; Sea & Reef and ORA were substituted as additional cross-verification sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive-bred at commercial scale by ORA and Sea & Reef. Demersal egg-layer; nest sizes of 500+ are common. Larvae need rotifer cultures followed by enriched brine shrimp in a dedicated rearing tank.
